topics.label: Repo Navigation
topics.intro: Main entry pages and the high-level structure of the project.
hints: Start from a page before diving into raw files. The repo is easier to navigate page-first than folder-first.
Use Topics when you want a course-like path, Notes when you want chapter references, and Visuals when you want intuition first.
If Maxwell returns too many results, narrow the question by source: from notes, from notebooks, from jackson, or from griffiths.

topics.label: Vector Analysis
topics.intro: Foundational calculus tools used throughout electrodynamics.
hints: Before computing anything, identify whether the question is about gradient, divergence, curl, line integrals, surface integrals, or volume integrals.
Check symmetry first. Many vector-analysis exercises collapse if the geometry forces one component or one coordinate dependence.
If an identity is requested, verify both sides by expanding components or by applying the relevant theorem with the correct orientation.

topics.label: Electrostatics
topics.intro: Charge, field, potential, and capacitance foundations.
study_paths: slides/Coulombs Law, Electric potential and capacitance.pdf
hints: Decide whether force, field, potential, or energy is the cleanest variable. Converting too early often makes the algebra worse.
Use Gauss's law only when symmetry is strong enough to determine the field from the flux integral.
When a conductor appears, separate what is fixed by electrostatic equilibrium from what still must be solved from boundary data.

topics.label: Dirac Delta
topics.intro: Distribution-based notes, problems, and interactive intuition builders.
hints: Always ask what object the delta is acting on. The delta is defined by how it samples a test function under an integral.
Match the dimensionality correctly: one-dimensional, surface, and volume deltas are not interchangeable.
If coordinates change, include the Jacobian factor. Most mistakes come from forgetting the measure attached to the integral.

topics.label: Poisson Equation
hints: Write the source first and only then choose coordinates. The coordinate system should make the source and boundaries simpler, not just the Laplacian prettier.
Separate the problem into equation plus boundary conditions. Poisson's equation alone is not enough to determine a unique solution.
Check the source-free limit. If setting rho to zero does not reduce your setup to Laplace's equation cleanly, something upstream is wrong.

topics.label: Laplace Equation
hints: Start by identifying the geometry and which coordinates allow separation of variables or symmetry reduction.
In source-free regions, uniqueness theorems are your safety check. If two candidate answers satisfy the same boundary data, only one can survive.
Use boundary behavior to kill nonphysical terms early, especially singular terms or modes that blow up in the wrong region.

topics.label: Method of Images
topics.intro: Image-charge intuition, grounded conductors, and boundary matching.
hints: State the physical region and the boundary condition before placing any image charge.
Every image charge must live outside the physical region. Its job is only to enforce the boundary exactly.
After constructing the image system, verify the boundary explicitly and compute forces using only real charges acted on by image fields.

topics.label: Green's Functions
topics.intro: Kernel methods for electrostatic boundary-value problems.
hints: Identify the operator, the source term, and the boundary condition before writing any Green function.
The free-space kernel is only the starting point. The boundary-adapted Green function must reflect the geometry of the problem.
When using the final integral representation, track carefully which terms come from volume sources and which come from boundary data.

topics.label: Multipole Expansion
hints: Choose the expansion center first. A bad origin can hide symmetry and make lower moments appear unnecessarily.
Work in orders: monopole, dipole, quadrupole. Stop once the first nonzero moment that answers the question is identified.
Always check the regime of validity. Multipole expansions are asymptotic in the far field, not exact everywhere.

topics.label: Dielectrics
topics.intro: Electric fields in matter and polarization-based reasoning.
hints: Separate free charge from bound charge at the start. Mixing them usually breaks the boundary conditions.
Use D when free charge is the clean input, and use polarization P when the material response itself is the quantity of interest.
At interfaces, track which normal and tangential components are constrained. Most dielectric problems are won or lost there.

topics.label: Magnetostatics
topics.intro: Steady-current magnetic fields and magnetic matter.
hints: Decide whether the problem is easier from Biot-Savart, Ampere's law, vector potential, or magnetic scalar potential.
If matter is present, separate free current from bound current before applying constitutive relations.
Use symmetry aggressively. Magnetostatic integrals become much simpler once the surviving field direction is fixed.

topics.label: Electromagnetic Radiation
study_paths: slides/Coulombs Law, Electric potential and capacitance.pdf
hints: Separate near-zone intuition from radiation-zone behavior. They are governed by different distance scalings.
Track time dependence before simplifying the spatial structure. Retardation changes the logic of the problem.
If the question is about power or angular pattern, identify the dominant time-dependent multipole first.

topics.label: Waveguides
topics.intro: Guided modes, cutoff, and dispersion in bounded geometries.
hints: Impose the conducting-wall boundary conditions first. Allowed modes come from those constraints, not from the wave equation alone.
Separate longitudinal and transverse structure carefully. TE and TM labels refer to the vanishing longitudinal component.
Once the mode numbers are set, read cutoff and dispersion from the resulting separation constants.

topics.label: Visuals & Simulations
topics.intro: Interactive pages, animations, and computational intuition builders.
hints: Use visuals to test intuition, not to replace equations. Ask what invariant or boundary condition the animation is illustrating.
When a numerical result looks surprising, check domain, boundary conditions, and normalization before trusting the pattern.
If a simulation and an analytic result disagree, compare geometry and units first. Those mismatches are more common than physics failures.
